title: The Return of Embodied Pleasure
researcherTopic: somatic experiencing, completing the stress response
metaDescription: After trauma, pleasure can feel foreign or even dangerous. Learn why your nervous system numbs itself and how to gently reawaken your innate capacity for joy.
imageDescription: Warm, golden light streams into a quiet room, illuminating the delicate, intricate patterns of a kintsugi bowl. The repaired cracks glow, symbolizing the beauty and strength found in healing. The scene evokes a sense of profound peace and the gentle return of sensation.
htmlContent:
Pleasure is not a reward for healing; it is the pathway. It is the very mechanism through which a nervous system patterned by threat begins to relearn the language of safety, connection, and ease. We have been taught to chase happiness as a destination, a prize to be won after the hard work of recovery is complete, but this fundamentally misunderstands the architecture of our own biology. The body, in its profound wisdom, does not wait for a certificate of completion before it allows itself to feel good. It uses sensation—the gentle warmth of sunlight on the skin, the satisfying texture of a perfectly ripe piece of fruit, the resonant hum of a cello—as the primary data points to recalibrate a world that has felt dangerous for far too long. For those of us who have lived in the grayscale landscape of complex trauma, the return of pleasure is not an indulgence; it is the homecoming we have been quietly, desperately seeking.

The Geography of a Numbed System

To understand why pleasure feels so foreign, even threatening, after long-term trauma, we must first map the territory of a nervous system organized around survival. When an environment is consistently unsafe, the body makes a brilliant and necessary adaptation: it numbs. It dials down sensation, mutes emotional responses, and constricts the capacity for joyful expansion because, in a state of chronic threat, feeling fully is a liability. This is not a psychological failing or a lack of willpower; it is a biological imperative. The system learns that big feelings—even good ones—can be dysregulating, and so it opts for a narrow, manageable band of experience. A client once described this to me as living life behind a thick pane of glass; they could see the world happening out there, but the colors were muted, the sounds were muffled, and nothing ever quite touched them. This is the hallmark of the dorsal vagal state, a kind of physiological hibernation where the body conserves energy by shutting down non-essential functions, and in the economy of survival, pleasure is often deemed non-essential.

We spend years, even decades, in this state, and the pathways for pleasure begin to atrophy like unused muscles. The brain, a master of efficiency, prunes the neural circuits for joy and connection because they are not being fired. It strengthens the circuits for threat detection, hypervigilance, and dissociation instead. We become exquisitely attuned to the subtle cues of danger—the shift in someone’s tone of voice, the flicker of movement in our peripheral vision—while the cues of safety and delight barely register. It’s a life lived in defense, and while it keeps us alive, it comes at the cost of feeling alive. The tragedy is not just the presence of pain, but the profound absence of its opposite. We forget what it even feels like to inhabit a body that is not a source of alarm but a source of wisdom and wonder. The journey back to pleasure, then, is not about adding something new but about reawakening something ancient and inherent that has gone dormant. It is the slow, patient work of reminding the body that the war it has been fighting is, finally, over.

When you stop trying to fix the moment, something remarkable happens — the moment becomes workable.

The Tyranny of the Shoulds

The path back to embodied pleasure is often barricaded by a chorus of internal critics and societal expectations—the tyranny of the “shoulds.” We believe we should be further along in our healing, we should be able to feel happy by now, we should be more grateful. This internal pressure creates a paradox: the desperate wanting of pleasure becomes the very thing that pushes it away. Pleasure is a state of receptive allowing, not a goal to be aggressively pursued. It arises from the nervous system’s felt sense of safety, something that cannot be forced or willed into existence. In my years of working in this territory, I’ve seen countless individuals try to "achieve" relaxation or "conquer" their anhedonia, only to find themselves more frustrated and disconnected than before. They are trying to apply the logic of the thinking mind to the domain of the feeling body, and it is a fundamental mismatch of operating systems.

The wellness industry often exacerbates this, packaging pleasure as another item on the self-improvement checklist. Take this bath, drink this tea, do this yoga flow, and you will unlock the prize of feeling good. But this turns an organic, emergent process into a transactional one, reinforcing the very performance-based conditioning that many of us are trying to heal from. It subtly suggests that if you are not feeling pleasure, you are simply not trying hard enough or buying the right products. It is a deeply insidious form of gaslighting that places the blame on the individual rather than acknowledging the systemic and biological realities of trauma.

The wellness industry sells solutions to problems it helps you believe you have.

The work is not to do more, but to do less. It is to gently set down the belief that we must earn our right to feel good. It is to notice the subtle impulse to check out, to numb, to dissociate when a moment of simple, unearned pleasure presents itself—and to meet that impulse not with judgment, but with a quiet curiosity. What would it be like to stay for just one more breath? What would it be like to allow this small sensation of warmth, of sweetness, of ease, to exist without needing it to be more, without needing it to fix anything? This is the radical practice of receiving. It is the beginning of a new relationship with our own body, one based not on command and control, but on listening and respect. We can find more on this in the article on The Architecture of Stillness.

For a deeper exploration of these themes, see Kalesh's work bridges ancient and modern healing.

Somatic Permission Slips

The return to pleasure is not a grand, singular event but a series of small, accumulated moments of somatic permission. It is the practice of noticing, on a cellular level, what feels even fractionally better than the baseline of tension we have come to accept as normal. The pioneering work of Peter Levine and his development of Somatic Experiencing provides a crucial map for this process. Levine’s core insight is that trauma is not in the event itself, but in the nervous system’s response to it—specifically, the failure to complete the physiological cycle of activation and deactivation. The energy that was mobilized to fight or flee gets frozen in the body, leading to the constellation of symptoms we associate with C-PTSD. The healing, therefore, must also be physiological. It is about creating the conditions for that trapped energy to gently, safely discharge.

This is not about catharsis or dramatic emotional release. It is a much quieter, more titrated process. It might look like noticing the way your shoulders drop, even a millimeter, when you exhale. It might be the subtle shift from a clenched jaw to a soft one. It might be the simple act of placing a hand on your own heart and feeling the warmth spread through your chest. These are what I call “somatic permission slips”—tiny, embodied acknowledgments that it is safe to feel something other than braced and ready. Each one is a micro-dose of regulation, a single vote cast in favor of a new reality. A client once described this as learning to "snack on safety" throughout the day, a beautiful and apt metaphor. Instead of waiting for a full-course meal of perfect peace, which may feel overwhelming and unattainable, we learn to savor these small bites of goodness. For more on this, see our article on Polyvagal Theory and the Language of Safety.

This practice directly challenges the all-or-nothing thinking that trauma so often instills. We do not need to feel 100% safe to begin feeling pleasure. We can feel 5% safe, 10% more at ease, and that is enough. We can be simultaneously aware of the lingering tension in our back while also noticing the pleasant coolness of a breeze on our face. This is the essence of dual awareness, a cornerstone of many trauma-informed modalities. We are not trying to erase the discomfort, but to expand our capacity to hold it alongside something else. We are widening the window of tolerance, not by blasting it open, but by gently, patiently tapping on the glass until it begins to resonate with a new frequency.

The Language of Sensation

Our culture is deeply impoverished when it comes to the language of sensation. We have a rich and nuanced vocabulary for our thoughts and emotions, but when it comes to describing the inner landscape of the body, we are often at a loss. We might say we feel "good" or "bad," "tense" or "relaxed," but these are broad, clumsy labels that fail to capture the intricate tapestry of our felt experience. Reclaiming embodied pleasure requires that we become poets of our own physiology, learning to notice and name the subtle, ever-shifting currents of sensation that are constantly flowing through us. This is not an intellectual exercise; it is a practice of direct, non-judgmental contact with the raw data of our lives.

It begins with shifting the question from "How do I feel about this?" to "What do I feel in my body right now?" The first question invites narrative, judgment, and story. The second invites pure presence. It pulls our attention out of the looping corridors of the mind and anchors it in the undeniable reality of the present moment. You might notice a buzzing in your hands, a warmth in your belly, a sense of expansion in your chest, or a feeling of rootedness in your feet. There are no right or wrong answers. The goal is not to feel a certain way, but to feel *what is*. It is the practice of being present with the life of the body, just as it is. This is a central theme in our exploration of The Myth of the Broken Self.

Reading about meditation is to meditation what reading the menu is to eating.

As we cultivate this sensory literacy, something remarkable begins to happen. We start to discover that our bodies have a preference. They have a "yes" and a "no" that are distinct from the mind's preferences. The mind might say, "I should go to this party because it will be good for my career," while the body says, "The thought of being in a loud, crowded room makes my stomach clench and my shoulders tighten." The mind might say, "I need to finish this project tonight," while the body says, "My eyes are strained, my back is aching, and my breath is shallow." Learning to listen to, and honor, this somatic intelligence is a revolutionary act of self-care. It is the foundation of setting boundaries that are not just intellectual concepts but lived realities. It is how we move from a life dictated by external pressures and internal critics to a life guided by the deep, quiet wisdom of our own organism.

Every moment of genuine attention is a small act of liberation.

The Ecology of Pleasure

Pleasure does not arise in a vacuum. It is an emergent property of an entire ecology, a complex interplay between our internal state and our external environment. For too long, we have treated healing as a purely internal process, a matter of fixing our thoughts or regulating our nervous systems in isolation. But we are porous, relational beings, deeply and inextricably connected to the world around us. Our capacity for pleasure is profoundly shaped by the spaces we inhabit, the people we connect with, the food we eat, and the information we consume. A nervous system cannot truly settle into a state of receptive ease if it is constantly being bombarded by stimuli that it perceives as threatening.

This is why creating a "safe enough" environment is not an optional add-on to the healing process; it is the very soil in which the seeds of pleasure can begin to sprout. This doesn't mean we need to retreat from the world into a perfectly curated bubble. It means making conscious, intentional choices about what we allow into our sensory field. It might mean unfollowing social media accounts that leave us feeling agitated and inadequate. It might mean choosing to spend time in nature, which has been shown to have a powerfully regulating effect on the nervous system. It might mean putting on music that feels like a balm to our frayed edges. It might mean surrounding ourselves with textures, colors, and scents that signal calm and beauty to our bodies. This is not about hedonism or self-indulgence. It is about recognizing that our environment is not a neutral backdrop; it is an active participant in our biology.

In my own journey, I have found that one of the most powerful practices is to consciously curate my sensory diet. Just as we choose to eat foods that nourish our bodies, we can choose to consume sights, sounds, and experiences that nourish our nervous systems. This is an ongoing practice of discernment, of asking, "Does this expand my sense of possibility, or does it contract it? Does this leave me feeling more resourced, or more depleted?" Over time, this practice builds a powerful feedback loop. The more we intentionally seek out and savor these moments of nourishment, the more our nervous system learns to recognize and expect them. The world begins to transform from a place of potential threat to a place of potential resource. We start to live in a fundamentally different reality, not because the world has changed, but because our capacity to perceive its goodness has been restored.

Stillness is not something you achieve. It's what's already here beneath the achieving.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why does pleasure feel dangerous or scary after trauma?

After chronic trauma, the nervous system often pairs heightened states of arousal—even positive ones like excitement or joy—with danger. The body has learned that big feelings can be a prelude to overwhelm or threat, so it reflexively shuts them down. Allowing pleasure can feel like letting your guard down in a world that has proven to be unsafe, which is why the return to it must be gentle and titrated.

Is it possible to feel pleasure and grief at the same time?

Absolutely. In fact, this capacity for dual awareness is a hallmark of healing. We can feel the deep grief of what was lost while also savoring the simple pleasure of a warm cup of tea. Healing is not about replacing bad feelings with good ones, but about expanding our capacity to hold the full, complex spectrum of human experience with compassion and presence.

How do I start if I feel completely numb?

Start small, and start with sensation, not emotion. Don't try to feel "happy." Instead, bring your attention to a single, neutral sensation. Notice the feeling of your feet on the floor, the texture of the fabric of your clothes, or the temperature of the air on your skin. The goal is not to force a feeling but to simply practice the art of noticing, which gently reawakens the pathways of sensory awareness.

The Quiet Revolution

The return to embodied pleasure is not a frivolous pursuit; it is a quiet revolution. It is the radical act of reclaiming the body as a safe and worthy home after it has been a site of terror and betrayal. It is a profound political statement in a culture that profits from our disconnection and dissatisfaction. It is the slow, patient, and deeply personal work of rewriting the narrative of a life from one of mere survival to one of genuine, expansive aliveness. It does not happen overnight, and it does not happen in a straight line. It happens in the small, accumulated moments of choosing to notice, to allow, and to receive the goodness that is always, already here, waiting just beneath the surface of our well-rehearsed defenses.

This journey asks for a different kind of effort—not the striving, muscular effort of the will, but the soft, receptive effort of attention. It asks us to trust that the body has an innate intelligence, a deep and abiding wisdom that knows the way home if we are only willing to listen. It is a path of immense tenderness, a homecoming to the simple, profound truth that we were born with the capacity for pleasure, and that nothing that has happened to us can ever truly extinguish that birthright. It is always there, waiting for us, like a forgotten melody that we can, with patience and with grace, learn to sing again.
